cases.name: Edgewood ISD v. Kirby (Edgewood I)
cases.holding_one_line: The school-finance system violated the Article VII, §1 duty to maintain an "efficient" system: districts must have "substantially equal access to similar revenues per pupil at similar levels of tax effort."
cases.relevance: Establishes the affirmative state duty. When a policy erodes the local property-tax base unevenly (a homestead-exemption jump hits districts differently by home values), the state's efficiency obligation is what plaintiffs invoke.

cases.name: Edgewood ISD v. Kirby (Edgewood II)
cases.holding_one_line: The Legislature's first fix (SB 1) still failed efficiency — simply adding state money without restructuring how districts access revenue did not cure the constitutional defect.
cases.relevance: Directly on point for a revenue drop: the Court held the state cannot satisfy its duty by pouring money in; the structural relationship between local tax effort and available revenue must be fixed. A homestead cut that shifts the burden to state backfill invites this scrutiny.

cases.name: Carrollton-Farmers Branch ISD v. Edgewood ISD (Edgewood III)
cases.holding_one_line: An ad valorem tax becomes an unconstitutional statewide property tax (Art. VIII, §1-e) when "the State so completely controls the levy, assessment and disbursement of revenue … that the authority employed is without meaningful discretion"; the County Education District tax was struck.
cases.relevance: The core state-property-tax test. If the state raises the exemption and then leans on districts to tax to the cap to replace lost revenue, districts lose meaningful discretion — the exact trigger Edgewood III defines.

cases.name: Edgewood ISD v. Meno (Edgewood IV)
cases.holding_one_line: SB 7 (including recapture / "Robin Hood") did not violate Art. VII §1 or Art. VIII §1-e, but the system was "minimally acceptable only when viewed through the prism of history."
cases.relevance: Marks how little headroom the system has. The Court signaled the structure was already at its constitutional floor — any further erosion of local capacity (e.g., a large exemption increase) pushes it below that floor.

cases.name: Neeley v. West Orange-Cove Consolidated ISD
cases.holding_one_line: Local property taxes had become an unconstitutional state property tax because the State left districts "no meaningful discretion to tax below maximum rates"; adequacy claim rejected but the structural warning was renewed.
cases.relevance: The end of the lineage and the sharpest fit: when state policy forces districts to the rate ceiling to fund what the state mandates, the local tax is a de facto state tax. A homestead-exemption hike that erodes the base while spending mandates hold reopens this exact theory.

prop13.change: Increased the school-district homestead exemption from $100,000 to $140,000 of market value.

tac_rules.subject: Applications for Property Tax Exemptions — governs the residence-homestead exemption application and the forms the Comptroller prescribes (which state the exemption amount). Subchapter C, Appraisal District Administration.
Continuation of Residence Homestead Exemption While Replacement Structure is Constructed. Subchapter C.
Partial Exemption Lists — the appraisal-record rule for partial exemptions such as the residence homestead. Subchapter H, Tax Record Requirements.
Notice of Exemption Application Requirement. Subchapter H, Tax Record Requirements.
